title: First Contact Protocol
narrativeGoal: Sable detects the anomalous signal during routine relay maintenance. What begins as a diagnostic puzzle becomes something she can't classify. The scene establishes her isolation, her competence, and the first crack in both — something that doesn't fit her systems.
emotionalBeat: Professional curiosity sharpening into unease she refuses to name
readerEffect: Feel the weight of Sable's solitude before the signal arrives, then feel it change shape — loneliness becoming something with an audience.

wrongAbout: This is just a day-in-the-life space story

knows: It doesn't match any known protocol
suspects: Sable's loneliness is affecting her judgment
She's not going to report this immediately
wrongAbout: That the signal is just interference
activeTensions: How long has she really been alone?

characterKnowledgeChanges.sable: Discovers an anomalous signal pattern that doesn't match any catalogue entry. Her diagnostic tools can't classify it. For the first time in months, she doesn't know what she's looking at.

subtext.surfaceConversation: A systems engineer running diagnostics on an anomalous reading
subtext.actualConversation: A lonely person encountering something that might be listening
subtext.enforcementRule: Sable must frame everything technically. The loneliness enters through what she does, not what she thinks about doing.

sable: Talks aloud to herself in technical fragments
Her log entries are technically correct but emotionally transparent to the reader

pacing: Slow and rhythmic at first — the routine is the rhythm. When the signal appears, the rhythm breaks. Shorter paragraphs, more fragments.
sensoryNotes: The hum of the station. The difference between the expected sounds and a new one. The observation deck's particular quality of silence. Coffee cooling in a cup. Drone Six's compensating clicks.
sceneSpecificProhibitions: No exposition about how relay stations work — Sable knows, the reader figures it out
No awe at space — she's been here almost a year
No 'is anyone there?' moment — Sable is too professional for that
Do not describe the signal as 'beautiful' or 'haunting'

anchorLines.text: Station log, day 337. All systems nominal. I am not.
She ran the diagnostic three times. Three times it came back: UNCLASSIFIED. She'd never seen her own system say that.
anchorLines.placement: The moment the signal becomes real

chunkDescriptions: Day 337 — Sable's morning routine on Meridian. Log entry, coffee, drone check, the comfortable weight of solitude. Establish the rhythm that will break.
The anomaly — during routine relay maintenance, something appears in the signal data. Sable runs diagnostics. The results don't fit any pattern she knows.
The decision — Sable doesn't report the signal. Instead she saves it, labels it personal, and begins her own analysis. She tells herself it's professional curiosity. It isn't.
failureModeToAvoid: Making the signal arrival too dramatic. It should feel like a puzzle, not an event. The drama is internal — the crack in Sable's certainty about what she knows.
